title: Schema: failure_bundles table + Task.failure_bundle_id migration
context: Part of the First-Failure Evidence Bundle system. See design: docs/failure-evidence-bundle-design.md. The system captures a structured failure packet once per failed task to eliminate repeated diagnostic re-derivation. This handoff handles the data layer.

spec.follow_existing_pattern: See migrations/task_improvements_foundation.py for the add_column_if_missing pattern and CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS pattern.
migration_steps: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS failure_bundles with columns: id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, task_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tasks(id), worker_run_id INTEGER REFERENCES worker_runs(id), created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, state_timeline JSON NOT NULL, triggering_exception TEXT, first_failing_command TEXT, cancellation_reason TEXT, error_type TEXT, escalation_tier_at_failure INTEGER, retry_count_at_failure INTEGER, model_used TEXT, duration_seconds REAL, exit_code INTEGER
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS uq_failure_bundles_task_id ON failure_bundles(task_id)
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS ix_failure_bundles_task_id ON failure_bundles(task_id)

acceptance: Migration script runs idempotently (safe to run twice). SQLAlchemy model exists in app/models.py. Task model has failure_bundle_id column. pytest passes.

title: Implement FailureBundleBuilder service
context: Part of the First-Failure Evidence Bundle system. See design: docs/failure-evidence-bundle-design.md. H1 must be merged first (the DB table must exist). This handoff implements the core service that captures and persists failure bundles.

spec.method: async def capture(self, task_id: str, worker_run_id: int | None, error_log: str, exit_code: int, model: str | None, duration_seconds: float, session_key: str | None = None) -> str
spec.idempotency: SELECT FROM failure_bundles WHERE task_id=? before inserting. If row exists, return existing id without modification. This is the enforcement mechanism for first-failure-only semantics.
state_timeline_build: Load all WorkerRun rows WHERE task_id=? ORDER BY started_at ASC
- {ts: task.started_at, event: 'task_started', from_state: 'not_started', to_state: 'in_progress'} if task.started_at

- For each prior WorkerRun (succeeded=False, excluding current): {ts: run.started_at, event: 'prior_failure', worker_id: run.worker_id, model: run.model, error_type: run.timeout_reason or 'unknown', duration_seconds: (run.ended_at - run.started_at).total_seconds()}
- {ts: current_run.started_at, event: 'worker_spawned', worker_id: current_run.worker_id, model: model}
- {ts: now, event: 'worker_failed', from_state: 'in_progress', to_state: 'not_started', error_type: error_type, escalation_tier: task.escalation_tier}
spec.first_failing_command: OPTIONAL, best-effort. If session_key is provided and a transcript file can be located (reuse the existing _find_transcript_file approach from worker.py), scan assistant messages for the first tool_use block with a non-zero exit_code in stderr/stdout. Return the command string or None. Wrap in try/except — must not raise.
spec.error_type: Call classify_error_type(error_log) from app/orchestrator/worker_errors.py (or worker.py — find it)
spec.after_persist: UPDATE tasks SET failure_bundle_id=? WHERE id=? via db.execute() or db.get(Task, task_id); commit
spec.independent_session: Use the same _get_independent_session pattern as _record_worker_run in worker.py to avoid session conflicts — pass db session from constructor or accept db as param

acceptance: FailureBundleBuilder.capture() creates exactly one row per task_id (UNIQUE constraint enforces this)
Second call returns existing bundle ID, does not modify the row
state_timeline is a list of dicts with 'ts', 'event' keys, in chronological order
Task.failure_bundle_id is set after capture()
triggering_exception is truncated to 2000 chars
first_failing_command is None if transcript unavailable (does not raise)
Unit tests pass: see testing strategy in design doc

title: Hook FailureBundleBuilder into worker._handle_completion() failure path
context: Part of the First-Failure Evidence Bundle system. See design: docs/failure-evidence-bundle-design.md. H1 and H2 must be merged first. This handoff connects the capture service to the actual failure lifecycle in the orchestrator.

spec.location: In _handle_completion() — the `else` branch (failure path, around line 1086)
what_to_add: After the task is marked failed and committed (after AgentTracker.mark_failed, CircuitBreaker.record_failure, etc.):

Call await builder.capture(task_id=task_id, worker_run_id=<the run ID from _record_worker_run>, error_log=error_log, exit_code=exit_code, model=worker_info.model, duration_seconds=duration, session_key=worker_info.child_session_key)
Store returned bundle_id in a local var: failure_bundle_id
Pass failure_bundle_id through to escalation: include in the 'notes' context when escalation spawns retry/diagnostic tasks
spec.worker_run_id_availability: The WorkerRun is recorded by _record_worker_run() which is called separately. Look at when/where it's called relative to _handle_completion() — you may need to return the run ID from _record_worker_run() or query for the most recent WorkerRun for this task after it's committed.
spec.passing_to_escalation: In the escalation call (circuit_breaker.record_failure or wherever retry tasks are spawned), include failure_bundle_id in a context dict or in the task notes field: 'failure_bundle_id: {failure_bundle_id}'. The specific pattern depends on how retry tasks are created in this codebase — check escalation.py and circuit_breaker.py.
spec.graceful_degradation: Wrap the capture() call in try/except. If it fails, log a warning and continue — bundle capture must not block the main failure handling path.

acceptance: After a worker fails, a FailureBundle row exists in the DB for that task
Task.failure_bundle_id is set
A second failure on the same task does NOT create a duplicate bundle
If FailureBundleBuilder raises, the failure handling continues normally (logged as warning)
Integration test in tests/test_worker.py passes

title: Inject failure bundle into diagnostic prompts
context: Part of the First-Failure Evidence Bundle system. See design: docs/failure-evidence-bundle-design.md. H1-H3 must be merged first. This handoff makes diagnostic agents consume the bundle instead of re-deriving context.

what_to_add: In _spawn_diagnostic(), after loading the context_packet, load the FailureBundle for the triggering task: bundle = await db.execute(select(FailureBundle).where(FailureBundle.task_id == trigger.get('task_id'))).scalar_one_or_none()
In _build_prompt(), if bundle is not None, prepend this section to the prompt:
spec.graceful_degradation: If no bundle exists for this task (bundle is None), omit the section. Prompt still works without it.

acceptance: When a diagnostic is spawned for a task with a failure bundle, the prompt contains '## Failure Evidence Bundle' section with populated fields
When no bundle exists, the prompt is unchanged from current behavior
state_timeline is rendered as formatted JSON in the prompt
Test: mock a FailureBundle row, call _build_prompt(), assert section present and well-formed

title: Integrate failure bundle into FailureExplainerService
context: Part of the First-Failure Evidence Bundle system. See design: docs/failure-evidence-bundle-design.md. This handoff is dependent on the FailureExplainerService being implemented (see failure-explainer-handoffs.json). H1-H3 must also be merged. If FailureExplainerService does not yet exist, implement this as a stub that will be wired in when the service is built.

spec.where: In the main evaluation method that builds FailureExplanation
what_to_add: Load FailureBundle for task_id: bundle = await db.execute(select(FailureBundle).where(FailureBundle.task_id == task_id)).scalar_one_or_none()
If bundle exists, include in supporting_data:
Use bundle.error_type to fast-path certain rule checks — if error_type is already known, you can skip DB queries for those rules and set the failure code directly
spec.fast_path_examples: If bundle.error_type == 'session_timeout': the timeout_exceeded rule fires without needing to check WorkerRun.timeout_reason separately. If bundle.retry_count_at_failure >= 3: the repeated_failure rule fires without counting WorkerRun rows.

acceptance: GET /api/intelligence/tasks/{task_id}/failure-explanation includes 'failure_bundle' in supporting_data when a bundle exists
failure_bundle.state_timeline is present and is a list
When no bundle exists, supporting_data['failure_bundle'] is absent or null — no error
At least one rule check uses bundle data for fast-path (documented in code comment)
